breaking.text: S&P 500 closes down 1.3% at 6,740 — Wall Street posts worst week since October
Brent crude surges above $90/barrel on Iran conflict escalation; analysts warn of $100+ scenario
U.S. economy lost 92,000 jobs in February — worst payrolls print since early pandemic, unemployment at 4.4%
Dow plunges 642 points as stagflation fears grip Wall Street
WTI crude breaks above $90/barrel with 35% weekly gain — biggest since oil futures began trading in 1983

summary: U.S. markets suffered a broad sell-off on Friday as February nonfarm payrolls shocked Wall Street with a loss of 92,000 jobs versus expectations for a 56,000 gain, pushing unemployment to 4.4% — its highest since 2021. The S&P 500 fell 1.3% to 6,740.02, the Dow dropped 453 points (-0.9%) to 47,501.55, and the Nasdaq slid 1.6% to 22,387.68 as surging oil prices above $90/barrel on Iran-related geopolitical tensions compounded recession fears. Energy was the lone bright spot with Occidental Petroleum rising 3.3% and majors like Exxon and Chevron gaining, while individual winners included Marvell Technology (+10% on record AI chip earnings) and Day One Biopharmaceuticals (+65.6% on a $2.5B acquisition by Servier). The Russell 2000 was hit hardest, falling 2.3%, as risk-off sentiment dominated and investors fled to safe havens amid dual pressures of economic slowdown and elevated inflation concerns.

highlights.text: Dow Inc. (DOW) rises 4% after JPMorgan upgrades to overweight, bucking the broader market decline
Russell 2000 plunges 2.3% to 2,525 — small caps bear brunt of sell-off as Wall Street posts worst week since October
Marvell Technology (MRVL) jumps 10%+ on record Q4 earnings powered by AI chip demand boom — analysts raise consensus target to $114
Brent crude surges above $90/barrel on Iran conflict with analysts warning of $100-$150 range if supply disruptions persist — energy stocks are lone sector in the green
February payrolls shocked at -92,000 jobs vs +56,000 expected — unemployment rises to 4.4%, highest since 2021, fueling recession fears and Fed pivot speculation
Gap shares slid ~8% after Q4 earnings of $0.45/share narrowly missed the $0.46 analyst consensus, adding to the risk-off mood
Average hourly earnings rose 0.4% monthly with annual wage growth stuck at 3.8%, complicating the Fed's rate-cut path and deepening stagflation concerns
Day One Biopharmaceuticals soared 65.6% after French pharma giant Servier announced a $2.5 billion acquisition for glioma drug Ojemda
Marvell Technology (MRVL) surged ~15% after reporting record earnings and issuing exceptionally strong FY2027 revenue guidance driven by insatiable AI infrastructure demand
WTI crude oil broke above $90/barrel with a staggering 35% weekly gain amid escalating Middle East conflict — energy stocks XOM, CVX, and OXY rallied while airlines DAL and UAL fell over 2%
February nonfarm payrolls plunged to -92,000, the worst reading in four years, as the unemployment rate rose to 4.4% — dramatically below the +50,000 consensus and fueling recession fears
VIX spikes 12.3% to 23.75 as average hourly earnings rise 0.4% m/m despite job losses, creating a 'lose-lose' policy dilemma for the Fed
Financials lead declines with Goldman Sachs -3.4%, American Express -3.2%, JPMorgan -3.0% as recession fears mount; Consumer Staples and Materials each drop over 2%
Energy sector stands alone in the green — Occidental Petroleum +3.3%, Exxon and Chevron each up ~1% — as Brent crude hits 4-year highs near $88-$94/bbl on Middle East escalation
Day One Biopharmaceuticals rockets 65.6% after Servier announces $2.5 billion acquisition targeting glioma drug Ojemda
Marvell Technology surges ~15-22% after record Q4 earnings beat driven by AI hardware demand, receives fresh analyst upgrades with $114 consensus target
February payrolls contract by 92,000 jobs — massive miss vs +59K consensus — with unemployment ticking up to 4.4%, sparking stagflation fears ahead of March 19 Fed meeting
Costco (COST) drops 6.1% and HPE plunges 12% on earnings disappointments, while Walgreens jumps 7.5% on Sycamore Partners take-private deal
Brent crude surges 8.8% to $92.93 on escalating Iran conflict — energy stocks rally with Occidental Petroleum up 3.3%, Exxon and Chevron each gaining over 1%
Day One Biopharmaceuticals soars 65.6% after Servier announces $2.5 billion acquisition for its glioma drug Ojemda

summary_history.text: U.S. markets sold off sharply on March 6 as a toxic combination of collapsing employment data and surging oil prices stoked stagflation fears. February nonfarm payrolls came in at a shocking -92,000 — far below the +50,000 consensus — while the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% and average hourly earnings remained stubbornly elevated at 3.8% annually. The Dow fell 642 points (-1.32%) to 48,097, the S&P 500 lost 0.58% to 6,829, and the Nasdaq slipped 0.33% to 22,732, with energy the lone bright spot as WTI crude surged past $90/barrel on escalating Middle East tensions. Marvell Technology was a standout gainer, surging on blowout AI-driven earnings, while Day One Biopharmaceuticals soared 65% on a $2.5B acquisition deal.

U.S. stocks are selling off sharply on Friday after a devastating February jobs report showed the economy lost 92,000 jobs, far worse than the +59,000 consensus estimate, sending the unemployment rate to 4.4%. The S&P 500 is down roughly 1.6% to 6,830 while the Dow has plunged about 800 points, with stagflation fears intensifying as surging Middle East tensions push Brent crude toward $88-$94 per barrel alongside stubborn wage growth of 0.4% monthly. Energy is the sole advancing sector while financials, consumer staples, and materials bear the brunt of selling; bright spots include Marvell Technology surging on record AI-driven earnings and Day One Biopharmaceuticals rocketing 65% on an acquisition deal. The VIX has spiked 12.3% to 23.75 as markets brace for the Fed's March 19th decision, where policymakers face a difficult choice between supporting a weakening labor market and fighting oil-driven inflation.

Markets staged a dramatic midday reversal on March 6 after Fed Chair Powell characterized the economy as stable, recovering from steep morning losses driven by a shocking February jobs report showing 92,000 jobs lost and surging oil prices amid the Iran conflict. The S&P 500 clawed back from a 1.3% drop to trade up 0.6% near 5,770, with the Nasdaq leading at +0.7% fueled by semiconductor strength. Broadcom surged 8% on blockbuster AI revenue while Marvell jumped 22% on record earnings, contrasting with Costco's 6.1% decline on an earnings miss and HPE's 12% plunge on weak guidance. The energy sector was a standout as Brent crude spiked to $92.93, lifting Occidental Petroleum 3.3% and Exxon and Chevron over 1% each.

Markets staged a dramatic midday reversal on March 6 after Fed Chair Jerome Powell characterized the economy as stable and resilient, calming nerves rattled by a shockingly weak February jobs report that showed nonfarm payrolls falling 92,000 — far below the 50,000 growth expected — while unemployment ticked up to 4.4%. The S&P 500, which plunged as much as 1.3% at the open on the jobs data and rising oil prices from the widening Middle East conflict, has rebounded to trade up 0.6% near 5,770, led by a tech and semiconductor rally. Broadcom's blowout earnings (+8%, AI revenue up 77% YoY) and a Marvell analyst upgrade are powering the Nasdaq's 0.7% gain, while Costco (-6.1% on earnings miss), HPE (-12% on weak guidance and layoffs), and oil-sensitive financials like Goldman Sachs (-3.7%) weigh on the Dow.

U.S. stocks are under heavy pressure Friday morning as a disastrous February jobs report — the economy lost 92,000 jobs versus expectations for a 60,000 gain — collides with surging oil prices driven by the escalating U.S.-Iran conflict now in its seventh day. WTI crude has spiked above $86 and Brent is approaching $90 as the Strait of Hormuz faces near-total shipping disruption, with Qatar's energy minister warning prices could hit $150. S&P 500 futures fell roughly 0.5% premarket while the Fed rate-cut timeline has been pushed to October, with a 97% probability the central bank holds rates at its March meeting. On the earnings front, Broadcom beat Q1 estimates with $19.3B revenue and 106% AI revenue growth, while Marvell Technology rallied 12% premarket on strong AI-driven results.

U.S. stock futures are under pressure Friday morning with S&P 500 futures down 0.29%, Nasdaq 100 futures off 0.39%, and Dow futures declining 0.21% as markets brace for the February employment report amid an escalating Middle East crisis. The Iran-Israel conflict has entered its seventh day with Israel's 'Operation Epic Fury' targeting Tehran directly, triggering closure of the Strait of Hormuz and sending crude oil surging past $81/barrel — its highest level since mid-2024. Thursday's session saw the Dow plummet 784 points (1.6%) to 47,954 while the S&P 500 fell 0.6% to 6,831, failing at 6,900 resistance and erasing all 2026 gains. Investors now await the jobs report at 8:30 AM ET, where economists expect a sharp deceleration to just 60,000 new jobs, complicated by winter weather and a Kaiser Permanente healthcare workers' strike.

U.S. stock futures are trading lower Friday morning with S&P 500 futures down 0.29%, Nasdaq 100 futures off 0.39%, and Dow futures declining 0.21% as investors brace for the February employment report amid escalating Middle East tensions. Thursday's session was brutal — the Dow plunged 784 points (1.6%) to 47,954 while the S&P 500 fell 0.56% to 6,830.71 as crude oil surged over 8% on Iran's claim of striking an American oil tanker in the Persian Gulf. The February jobs report, due at 8:30 AM ET, is expected to show just 60,000 jobs added versus 130,000 in January, with the Kaiser Permanente strike affecting roughly 30,000 positions potentially distorting the data. Bright spots remain in semiconductor earnings with Broadcom jumping 4.8% after hours on a profit beat and Marvell projecting fiscal 2027 revenue approaching $11 billion, though Nvidia faces headwinds from potential AI chip export licensing restrictions.

S&P 500 futures are stabilizing Friday morning after a volatile week dominated by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran that sent oil prices surging and pushed the index negative for 2026. The S&P closed Thursday at 6,830.71 (down 0.56%) after failing to break through 6,900 resistance, with the VIX jumping to 22.08 — its highest since autumn 2025. WTI crude settled at $81.01 Thursday after an 8.5% single-day spike but is pulling back to $80.45 as the Trump administration signals intervention on energy prices. All eyes now turn to the February jobs report at 8:30 AM ET, where economists expect just 50,000 new payrolls — a weak print that could further push back already-diminished Fed rate cut expectations.
